messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":83,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"not applicable","city_of_living":"Naples","employment_status":"retired","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"widowed","children":4,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"creative","religious_beliefs":"Islam","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["cooking","volunteering","rock climbing","travel","yoga"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":3,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":25,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":99,"level":"high"},"agreeableness":{"score":33,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":82,"level":"high"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

  "opinion": "As an 83-year-old man from Naples, retired and widowed with four children, I spend about an hour daily on social media, and I've seen my share of fake content. Managing it is like navigating a bustling market—you need a keen eye and a bit of wisdom. My background in lower secondary education and my parents' limited schooling taught me to value practical knowledge over fancy theories, so I rely on common sense and life experience. Being a native-born Italian, I cherish our community's traditions and honesty, which makes fake news feel like a betrayal. My religious beliefs in Islam emphasize truth and integrity, so I take this issue seriously. With high extraversion, I enjoy connecting with others online, but my high neuroticism means fake content can really upset me, stirring anxiety about misinformation spreading. My low openness makes me skeptical of new, flashy claims, preferring trusted sources I've known for years. I manage fake content by first checking the source—if it's from a reputable news outlet or a friend I trust, I consider it more carefully. I also look for consistency; if a story seems too outrageous or contradicts what I know from my volunteering or travels, I pause and investigate further. Cooking has taught me patience, so I don't rush to share posts without verifying them. I use social media to stay in touch with family and friends, sharing photos from my yoga sessions or rock climbing adventures, but I avoid spreading unverified news. When I encounter fake content, I report it or simply ignore it, focusing on positive interactions instead. In short, dealing with fake content is about staying grounded, trusting your gut, and prioritizing real connections over sensational stories.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As an 83-year-old man from Naples, retired and widowed with four children, I spend about an hour daily on social media, and I've seen my share of fake content. Managing it is like navigating a bustling market—you need a keen eye and a bit of wisdom. My background in lower secondary education and my parents' limited schooling taught me to value practical knowledge over fancy theories, so I rely on common sense and life experience. Being a native-born Italian, I cherish our community's traditions and honesty, which makes fake news feel like a betrayal. My religious beliefs in Islam emphasize truth and integrity, so I take this issue seriously. With high extraversion, I enjoy connecting with others online, but my high neuroticism means fake content can really upset me, stirring anxiety about misinformation spreading. My low openness makes me skeptical of new, flashy claims, preferring trusted sources I've known for years. I manage fake content by first checking the source—if it's from a reputable news outlet or a friend I trust, I consider it more carefully. I also look for consistency; if a story seems too outrageous or contradicts what I know from my volunteering or travels, I pause and investigate further. Cooking has taught me patience, so I don't rush to share posts without verifying them. I use social media to stay in touch with family and friends, sharing photos from my yoga sessions or rock climbing adventures, but I avoid spreading unverified news. When I encounter fake content, I report it or simply ignore it, focusing on positive interactions instead. In short, dealing with fake content is about staying grounded, trusting your gut, and prioritizing real connections over sensational stories.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (83) implies life experience and caution; gender and sexual orientation not heavily weighted; occupation (retired) suggests time for reflection; city (Naples) influences community values; education (lower secondary) leads to practical approach; marital status (widowed) and children (4) emphasize family trust; migration (native-born Italian) fosters cultural pride; psychological feature (creative) adds nuanced thinking; religion (Islam) stresses truth; time on social media (1 hour daily) indicates limited but engaged use; hobbies like volunteering and travel provide real-world context; OCEAN traits: low openness (skepticism), low conscientiousness (less structured but adaptable), high extraversion (social engagement), moderate agreeableness (balanced judgment), high neuroticism (anxiety about misinformation). Tone is conversational, reflective, and grounded, avoiding technical jargon, with examples from daily life like cooking and volunteering.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational, reflective, and grounded, with a mix of wisdom and concern, avoiding formal or technical language.
